key_points: In vitro study comparing conventional irrigation (CI), passive ultrasonic irrigation (PUI), and Er:YAG laser-activated irrigation (LAI) in both conventional (CIA) and minimally invasive access (MIA) cavities using extracted maxillary first molars infected with E. faecalis.

Both PUI and LAI significantly reduced bacterial CFU counts compared to conventional irrigation alone (P < 0.05); no significant difference was found between PUI and LAI in root canal lumen disinfection.
In dentin debris analysis after enlargement to F3, LAI (group C) showed the least bacterial CFU counts, suggesting superior penetration into dentinal tubules compared to PUI.

Minimally invasive access with conventional irrigation (group 2A) performed better than conventional access with conventional irrigation (group 1A), possibly due to the preservation of coronal dentin maintaining irrigant pressure.
No significant difference was found between CIA and MIA groups when PUI or LAI was used, suggesting that both techniques can compensate for the access limitations of minimally invasive cavities.
Er:YAG LAI was noted as easier to operate and more suitable for minimally invasive root canal treatment due to its effectiveness in dentinal tubule disinfection.

outcomes.measured: Bacterial CFU counts in root canal lumen (paper tip method)
outcomes.result: PUI and LAI both significantly reduced CFU vs CI (P < 0.05); no significant difference between PUI and LAI (P > 0.05)
outcomes.clinical_significance: Both PUI and LAI are more effective than conventional irrigation for root canal lumen disinfection regardless of access type

outcomes.measured: Bacterial CFU counts in dentin debris (F3 file debris)
outcomes.result: LAI group had the least CFU in dentin debris; significant antagonism between access type and disinfection method (F=7.224, P=0.002)
outcomes.clinical_significance: Er:YAG LAI appears superior to PUI for removing bacteria from dentinal tubules

outcomes.measured: Comparison of CIA vs MIA with conventional irrigation
outcomes.result: MIA + CI (group 2A) was significantly better than CIA + CI (group 1A) (P < 0.05)
outcomes.clinical_significance: Minimally invasive access may enhance irrigant effectiveness when used with conventional irrigation alone

outcomes.measured: Comparison of CIA vs MIA with PUI or LAI
outcomes.result: No significant difference between group 1B vs 2B or group 1C vs 2C (P > 0.05)
outcomes.clinical_significance: PUI and LAI can overcome the disinfection limitations associated with minimally invasive access cavities

notes: Study used ProTaper Universal F2 for initial instrumentation and F3 for dentin debris collection. E. faecalis infection model. Computer-guided minimally invasive access was used for MIA group. Paper tip method used for microbial sampling.

population.condition: Extracted maxillary first molars infected with E. faecalis

superiority_claim: El autor afirma que 'Er:YAG laser is better than ultrasonic in removing bacteria from dentinal tubules and is easy to operate, which is more suitable for minimally invasive root canal treatment.'
source_citation: Shan, X., Tian, F., Li, J., Yang, N., Wang, Y., & Sun, H. (2022). Comparison of Er:YAG laser and ultrasonic in root canal disinfection under minimally invasive access cavity. Lasers in Medical Science. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10103-022-03613-0
clinical_indication: Root canal disinfection in minimally invasive and conventional access cavities

drugs_mentioned.name: sodium hypochlorite
drugs_mentioned.dental_relevance: Used as the irrigant activated by Er:YAG laser and ultrasonic in root canal disinfection protocols
drugs_mentioned.laser_implication: NaOCl is the irrigant activated by Er:YAG LAI; photoacoustic/cavitation effects enhance its penetration into dentinal tubules

uncertainties: Specific Er:YAG laser parameters (power, energy per pulse, frequency, pulse duration, fiber tip type) are not reported in the abstract; full text may contain these details but they are not available here.
The specific NaOCl concentration used as irrigant is not mentioned in the abstract.
The exact LAI technique (fiber insertion depth, activation time, number of cycles) is not described in the abstract.
Brand/model of Er:YAG laser used is not specified.
Results are from an in vitro E. faecalis model; clinical translation to in vivo conditions is uncertain.

protocols.name: Er:YAG Laser-Activated Irrigation (LAI) en Acceso Mínimamente Invasivo
synonyms_es: irrigación activada por láser Er:YAG endodoncia
activación de irrigante con láser de erbio
LAI endodóntico con acceso mínimamente invasivo
irrigación láser en cavidad de acceso conservadora
protocols.purpose: Activar el irrigante endodóntico (NaOCl) mediante pulsos de láser Er:YAG para mejorar la desinfección del sistema de conductos radiculares, incluyendo los túbulos dentinarios, en cavidades de acceso mínimamente invasivas o convencionales.

parameters_table.equipment_class: Cualquier Er:YAG 2940 nm con fibra intracanal para activación de irrigante (LAI)

critical_notes: Los parámetros técnicos específicos del protocolo (potencia, energía por pulso, frecuencia, tipo de fibra, tiempo de activación) no están reportados en el paper original; se requiere consultar el texto completo para obtener estos datos.
El estudio es in vitro con modelo de E. faecalis en molares maxilares extraídos; la extrapolación clínica directa debe hacerse con cautela.

LAI con Er:YAG mostró ventaja sobre PUI en la eliminación de bacterias de los túbulos dentinarios (debris de dentina), aunque no hubo diferencia significativa en la desinfección del lumen del conducto.

El acceso mínimamente invasivo guiado por computadora (MIA) con LAI o PUI logró resultados equivalentes al acceso convencional, lo que sugiere que la activación del irrigante puede compensar las limitaciones geométricas del acceso conservador.
El documento presenta esta técnica favorablemente; existen alternativas (PUI, irrigación sónica, PIPS™, SWEEPS™) con mecanismos de acción diferentes que pueden ser apropiadas según el caso clínico.
technique_steps: Instrumentar el conducto radicular hasta el tamaño deseado (en este estudio, ProTaper Universal F2 para la fase de desinfección inicial).
Establecer el acceso (convencional o mínimamente invasivo guiado por computadora según el caso).
Introducir el irrigante (NaOCl) en el conducto radicular.
Insertar la fibra del láser Er:YAG en el conducto e iniciar la activación del irrigante mediante pulsos láser.
Retirar muestras microbiológicas con puntas de papel para verificar la desinfección.
Ampliar el conducto a F3 y recolectar debris dentinario para evaluación adicional si se requiere.
indications: Desinfección de conductos radiculares en acceso mínimamente invasivo
Desinfección de conductos radiculares en acceso convencional
Casos con infección por E. faecalis u otros microorganismos resistentes
Tratamiento endodóntico en molares maxilares con anatomía compleja
contraindications: Conductos con curvatura severa que impida la inserción segura de la fibra láser
Ausencia de irrigante en el conducto durante la activación (riesgo de daño térmico)
protocols.evidence_level: Bajo — estudio in vitro con modelo de E. faecalis en dientes extraídos (n=66); no hay datos clínicos en pacientes.
